questions.question_text: What is the primary purpose of California's Real Estate Recovery Fund?
questions.correct_answer: Compensate victims harmed by licensee fraud when the licensee cannot pay
distractors: Reimburse the DRE for investigation and prosecution costs
Fund continuing education grants for low-income licensees
Pay disciplinary fines imposed on suspended brokers
questions.explanation: The Recovery Fund compensates consumers who are victims of a licensee's fraud, misrepresentation, or deceit when the licensee is unable to satisfy a civil judgment.

questions.question_text: How is California's Real Estate Recovery Fund primarily funded?
questions.correct_answer: A portion of license fees paid by all licensees
distractors: Appropriations from the state general fund
Fines collected from disciplinary proceedings
Mandatory contributions from real estate trade associations
questions.explanation: The Recovery Fund is funded through a portion of the license fees paid by real estate licensees. This ensures the industry collectively supports a consumer protection backstop.

questions.question_text: A buyer sues a broker for fraud and wins a $60,000 judgment. The broker has no assets. What must the buyer do before claiming from the Recovery Fund?
questions.correct_answer: Get a civil judgment, attempt collection, and then file within one year of discovery
distractors: File a DRE complaint and wait for the disciplinary outcome
Notify the Real Estate Advisory Commission and request mediation
Submit proof of loss directly to the DRE without a court judgment
questions.explanation: To claim from the Recovery Fund, a victim must first obtain a civil court judgment against the licensee, make a good-faith effort to collect, fail to collect, and then file a claim within one year of discovering the loss (with a three-year outer limit).

questions.question_text: A buyer discovers on March 1, 2025, that a broker defrauded her in a 2022 transaction. By when must she file a Recovery Fund claim?
distractors: March 1, 2028 (three years after discovery)
December 31, 2025 (end of discovery year)
March 1, 2025 (date of discovery)
questions.explanation: A Recovery Fund claim must be filed within one year of discovering the fraud. Since discovery was March 1, 2025, the claim deadline is March 1, 2026. The three-year limit runs from the acts themselves, not discovery.

questions.question_text: A buyer claims the Recovery Fund should cover her loss because a broker who was acting as her friend — not in any professional capacity — defrauded her during a loan. Is this claim valid?
questions.correct_answer: No, the Recovery Fund only covers acts performed in a licensed capacity
distractors: Yes, any fraud by a licensee qualifies regardless of capacity
Yes, as long as the perpetrator held an active license at the time
No, only sellers can claim from the Recovery Fund
questions.explanation: The Recovery Fund covers only losses caused by a licensee acting in their licensed real estate capacity. Personal or non-licensed conduct, even by a licensee, is not covered.

questions.question_text: What is the maximum Recovery Fund payout per single transaction in California?
questions.explanation: California limits Recovery Fund payments to $50,000 per transaction, regardless of the actual judgment amount. Multiple victims in the same transaction share this cap.

questions.question_text: A broker defrauds clients across five separate transactions over three years. What is the maximum total Recovery Fund liability for this broker?
distractors: $50,000 total regardless of number of transactions
$500,000 if more than four transactions are involved
Unlimited — each victim is entitled to the full $50,000
questions.explanation: The Recovery Fund caps total payouts at $250,000 per licensee over their lifetime, regardless of how many transactions or victims are involved. Per-transaction the limit is $50,000.

questions.question_text: After the Recovery Fund pays a claim on behalf of a broker, what happens to the broker's license?
questions.correct_answer: The license is automatically suspended until the Fund is repaid with interest
distractors: The license is restricted to buyer representation only
The license is permanently revoked with no right of appeal
The broker receives a formal reprimand but retains the license
questions.explanation: When the Recovery Fund pays out on a licensee's behalf, that licensee's license is automatically suspended. The license cannot be reinstated until the full amount — plus interest — is repaid to the Fund.

questions.question_text: A broker's license is suspended after the Recovery Fund pays $40,000 to a defrauded buyer. The broker repays $20,000 and asks the DRE to reinstate her license. What happens?
questions.correct_answer: The license remains suspended until the full $40,000 plus interest is repaid
distractors: The license is reinstated since more than half was repaid
Reinstatement requires only repayment; interest is waived for prompt payment
The DRE may grant a restricted license after 50% repayment
questions.explanation: California law requires full repayment of the Recovery Fund payout plus interest before the suspended license can be reinstated. Partial repayment does not entitle the broker to reinstatement.

questions.question_text: Three buyers are defrauded by the same broker in one transaction and jointly suffer a $150,000 loss. How much can they collectively recover from the Fund?
questions.correct_answer: $50,000 total for the transaction, shared among the claimants
distractors: $150,000 — the full amount of their verified loss
$50,000 each, up to a total of $150,000
$25,000 each, regardless of individual loss
questions.explanation: The $50,000 cap applies per transaction, not per victim. All claimants arising from the same transaction share the $50,000 maximum, so these three buyers would divide $50,000 among themselves.
